video_title: Hawks Versus Doves Debate At The Fed
gist: Ed Yardeni argues the Fed's June FOMC may shift to a neutral-to-tightening bias, contrarian to consensus rate-cut hopes, because inflation has been above 2% for five years, AI capex is raising the neutral real rate, and the labor supply is tightening from immigration curbs and boomer retirements. He frames the recent dip as a Mag-7 rotation rather than a correction, reaffirms the 'Roaring 2020s' thesis (S&P 10,000 by 2029, possibly sooner), and flags 24% long-term earnings growth estimates as a sign of irrational exuberance.

insights.claim: The June FOMC statement is likely to shift from an easing bias to at least a neutral stance, and possibly a tightening bias, rather than the rate cuts consensus had been pricing.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Contrarian to rate-cut positioning; a hawkish tilt would pressure rate-sensitive equities and the front end of the curve.
insights.quote: the perception is that uh it uh it may not uh be biased towards easing, uh but it might be biased into a neutral stance. Uh we're still kind of holding on to the idea that it might actually be biased towards a tightening stance.

insights.claim: Because inflation has run above the Fed's 2% target for five consecutive years, the Fed has not actually been restrictive enough, undercutting the case for cuts.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Frames the rate debate around a credibility-failure argument that supports higher-for-longer rates.
insights.quote: its target is 2%. They have They've been above that for 5 years now. And so uh ... the Fed hasn't been restrictive enough.

insights.claim: AI capital spending is raising the neutral/normal real Fed funds rate, so the current actual Fed funds rate may be slightly too easy rather than restrictive.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: If the neutral rate has risen, markets are wrong to expect cuts; supports a structurally higher rate regime and elevated long yields.
insights.quote: the neutral or neutral Fed funds rate at which all this can happen is actually higher than otherwise would be the case. If that's so, then ... the actual Fed funds rate, isn't really even slightly restrictive. It might be actually a bit too easy

insights.claim: The recent oil-driven uptick in inflation has lowered the real Fed funds rate, making policy effectively easier just as the Fed should stay vigilant.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Reinforces the hawkish read; a falling real rate argues against near-term cuts.
insights.quote: This rebound in inflation, at least in a short-term basis, lowers the real Fed funds rate.

insights.claim: The dual-mandate risk balance has tilted clearly toward inflation rather than unemployment, weakening the case for a neutral stance.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Risk asymmetry supports a hawkish bias and a steeper-for-longer policy path.
insights.quote: now the risks clearly are more on the inflation side than they are on the unemployment rate side.

insights.claim: Supercore inflation appears stuck around 3.5%, well above pre-pandemic levels, signaling persistent underlying price pressure.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A sticky 3.5% supercore is the strongest data-based argument against imminent cuts.
insights.quote: supercore inflation just seems to be stuck here around 3 and 1/2%. And that's well above what we had back before before the pandemic.

insights.claim: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh is positioned as a leading dove arguing productivity gains will contain inflation, a view Yardeni warns the bond market may reject.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: If a dovish Warsh-led Fed under-tightens, long-bond yields could rise on lost inflation-fighting credibility.
insights.quote: if they indicate that uh they're buying into Warsh's idea that no need to worry about inflation, productivity is going to make a difference, the bond market may uh uh may not buy yields may go up

insights.claim: Markets began pricing rate hikes for late this year or early next year after the strong June 5th payroll report, the third strong month in a row.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Confirms the market has already started flipping from cuts to hikes, validating the hawkish thesis.
insights.quote: the market started to uh price in rate rate hikes instead of uh rate cuts. However, the rate hikes ... probabilities are scheduled for later this year and uh or early next year.

insights.claim: Trump's tariffs did not boost inflation outright but prevented it from falling to 2%, which would otherwise have been reached last year.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Nuanced framing: tariffs are a disinflation-blocker, not an inflation-driver; their fade-out reopens the path toward 2%.
insights.quote: the tariffs what they did is they didn't boost inflation, they just kept it from going lower. ... So we would have been at 2% by now if it hadn't been for the the tariffs.

insights.claim: Tariff-driven durable goods inflation appears to have peaked, with May data showing it no longer exerting the disinflationary drag of the prior year.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A turn in durable goods inflation is a leading tell on whether core CPI resumes its glide toward 2%.
insights.quote: now in the latest May data, there's some indications that that's maybe played itself out

insights.claim: The current oil spike differs from 2022 because supply-chain disruptions are far milder and there is no wage-price spiral, with wage growth having normalized.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Argues the oil shock is less inflationary than 2022, limiting its second-round effects and supporting the no-recession base case.
insights.quote: we're not we're not looking like like what we had back here with the wage price spiral.

insights.claim: Crude peaked around $118 twice during the war and fell to $87 by Friday, far below the $150 some Exxon and Chevron officials warned of, partly because Chinese oil demand was weaker than expected.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Falling oil removes a key headline-inflation risk and supports lower gasoline CPI ahead; weak China demand is a global growth tell.
insights.quote: we only spiked up twice at to about 118. And now as of uh uh Friday, we're down to 87

insights.claim: Falling gasoline futures point to lower national retail gas prices and a coming decline in CPI gasoline.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A near-term disinflation tailwind on headline CPI to watch in upcoming prints.
insights.quote: gasoline futures prices are coming down. That means that the national retail price is going to come down, and then the CPI gasoline should should come down.

insights.claim: AI capital spending, while disinflationary long-term via productivity, is actively inflationary now by boosting commodity, electricity, computer and semiconductor prices.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Counters the consensus that AI is purely disinflationary; near-term AI buildout is a source of inflation and a tailwind for power and commodity plays.
insights.quote: we agree that it's a productivity enhancer, and that it's going to lower inflation, but for the year and a half, AI capital spending is actually quite inflationary. It's boosted commodity prices, it's boosted uh electricity prices.

insights.claim: Helium supply fears tied to the Strait of Hormuz closure (relevant to semiconductor manufacturing) proved unfounded according to industrial gas suppliers.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Defuses a niche supply-chain scare for chipmakers; no material disruption materialized.
insights.quote: there there never really was a serious problem along those uh lines.

insights.claim: Grain and agricultural prices are falling on Middle East peace expectations, though fertilizer disruption remains a potential food-price risk.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Falling grain prices ease food-inflation fears; fertilizer is the residual wildcard to monitor.
insights.quote: agricultural prices, particularly grain prices, are falling with this news that the war in the Middle East may in fact uh be be be over.

insights.claim: Labor supply is tightening from the supply side as foreign-born labor force growth slows due to immigration policy and as 55-plus participation, especially men, declines on retirement.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Shrinking labor supply against firming demand could revive wage pressure and reinforce the higher-for-longer rate case.
insights.quote: the supply of labor is is tightening, the demand seems to be picking up, and that increases the chances that maybe wages will start start start moving up.

insights.claim: Unit labor costs have fallen to 0.5% year-over-year because productivity is now offsetting wage gains, unlike the 2021-22 surge.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Low unit labor costs are a key disinflation anchor; a reversal would be an early warning for margins and inflation.
insights.quote: unit labor costs are down to 0.5% on a on a year-over-year basis.

insights.claim: There is currently little wage premium for switching jobs, indicating job-switcher and job-stayer wage growth have converged and labor-market heat has cooled.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A narrow switcher premium signals contained wage inflation for now, even as supply tightens.
insights.quote: there's a much incentive to switch jobs these days. You don't really get much of a pay pay boost.

insights.claim: One-year-ahead inflation expectations from the NY Fed consumer survey spiked but remain comparatively calm and anchored.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Anchored expectations are the buffer preventing the oil shock from becoming a broader inflation problem.
insights.quote: you can see it shot up pretty dramatically here. Even the three year went up. But so far so good. This is pretty pretty calm.

insights.claim: S&P 500 forward earnings hit an all-time record $366 per share, with 2026 and 2027 analyst estimates still marching higher.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Record and rising forward earnings ('FEMO') are the core fundamental driver keeping the bull market intact.
insights.quote: forward earnings are up to $366 a share, all-time record high. The analyst expectations for 2027 just keeps marching higher

insights.claim: Analysts' long-term earnings growth (LTEG) estimate has surged to ~24% annually, which Yardeni calls 'loony tune' and a sign of irrational exuberance on the earnings side.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Unusually, the froth is in earnings estimates rather than P/E; a warning that analyst optimism may need to deflate.
insights.quote: long-term earnings growth has kind of shot up here uh, to uh, 24%. ... And it's uh, obviously loony tune.

insights.claim: Aggregating individually plausible analyst 'moon shot' estimates produces a nonsensical aggregate, since 24% S&P earnings growth for 3-5 years is implausible.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Highlights a bottom-up aggregation bias investors should discount when relying on consensus growth estimates.
insights.quote: when you put all those moon shots together you get a moon shot that just doesn't make any sense.

insights.claim: S&P 500, 400 (midcap) and 600 (smallcap) forward earnings are all at record highs, indicating broad-based earnings strength beyond megacaps.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Broad earnings breadth supports a market rally that is not solely megacap-dependent.
insights.quote: FEMO S&P 500 forward earnings. Uh, the 400 and the and the 600 all at the record highs.

insights.claim: The June dip is better characterized as a rotation out of the Magnificent Seven into the broader market than as a correction.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Argues for tilting toward the equal-weight / ex-mega-cap 'impressive 493' rather than de-risking equities.
insights.quote: I kind of view this June soon as more of a rotation than a correction. And I think it may still be going on here.

insights.claim: Year-to-date, the ex-Magnificent-Seven basket is up 12.6%, the S&P 500 up 8.6%, and the Magnificent Seven down 1.6%, evidencing the rotation.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Quantifies leadership shifting to the broad market; favors equal-weight exposure over cap-weighted megacap concentration.
insights.quote: year to date X mags up 12.6 S&P 500 8.6 and then minus 1.6.

insights.claim: Yardeni reaffirms the Roaring 2020s thesis with S&P 500 reaching 10,000 by end of 2029, and now wonders whether it could arrive before then.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A standing bullish price target implying substantial upside; the 'sooner' musing signals rising conviction.
insights.quote: I'm starting to wonder whether uh uh 10,000 might occur between before 2029.

insights.claim: SpaceX's private raise of ~$75 billion is described as just an entry tranche, with far more stock expected to be sold to the market later.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Signals a large future equity supply event; the deal's success reflects abundant risk appetite.
insights.quote: 75 billion is what was raised ... that number um is kind of the uh the entry price the the entry uh funds raised by SpaceX because uh at some point here uh there'll be a lot more stock

insights.claim: Yardeni is skeptical of SpaceX's Mars ambition and space-based data centers (citing space-junk risk) and would advise Tesla to build hybrids.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: A bearish read on the most speculative parts of the Musk narrative; flags hybrids as a Tesla product gap.
insights.quote: get get get me 5 minutes with Musk and I'll tell him there's really no reason to go to Mars. There's absolutely nothing there. Uh there's no reason to put uh data centers in outer space.

insights.claim: The bull market has now passed a long series of stress tests (pandemic, supply shocks, 2022 bear market, 2023 mini banking crisis, tariffs, Middle East war) without a recession, with real GDP at a record high.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Reinforces resilience narrative; each survived shock strengthens the case to stay invested.
insights.quote: the stress test has been passed so far remarkably well.

insights.claim: Gold's pullback to ~$4,100 near the $4,000 level (and the 200-day moving average channel) is viewed as a likely support and buying opportunity, not a breakdown.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Defines an actionable technical level: $4,000 as the line between buy-the-dip and further downside.
insights.quote: Next stop is 4,000 and if it holds that, that's probably a buying opportunity and so far so good.

insights.claim: A de-fanged Iran combined with the Abraham Accords and reasserted US military primacy could further strengthen the Roaring 2020s secular bull narrative.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: An upside geopolitical scenario for risk assets, though Yardeni stresses the thesis only needs growth without recession.
insights.quote: I'll take any argument that supports our roaring 2020s scenario. ... All I really need is for the economy to continue to grow without a without a recession

insights.claim: The end of the Middle East war removed the oil/jet-fuel scarcity fear and reversed crude from spike levels, with potential additional downside as strategic petroleum reserves are rebuilt.
insights.speaker: Ed Yardeni
insights.so_what: Peace plus SPR rebuilding creates a two-sided oil setup: downside on demand fears, eventual support from reserve buying.
insights.quote: there's going to be a lot of demand to increase strategic petroleum reserves again.
